title: Git Version Control
overview: Essential Git concepts and commands required for SDETs to manage test automation codebases collaboratively.
whyItMatters: Version control is critical for team collaboration, tracking changes, and maintaining code history. A strong grasp of Git enables seamless CI/CD integration and code reviews.
explanation: Git is a distributed version control system. A typical workflow involves cloning a repository, creating a feature branch, making commits, pulling the latest remote changes to resolve conflicts, and pushing the branch to a remote server (like GitHub or Bitbucket) to open a Pull Request. Important concepts include branching strategies (e.g., GitFlow) and understanding the working directory, staging area, and local repository.
keyPoints: Commit often with meaningful commit messages.
Always pull/rebase before pushing to avoid merge conflicts.
.gitignore is essential to prevent committing binaries, logs, and sensitive data.
Feature branches isolate work without disrupting the main codebase.
codeExample:
# Typical SDET daily workflow
git checkout main
git pull origin main
git checkout -b feature/login-tests
# ... make code changes ...
git add .
git commit -m "Add automated tests for login flow"
git push origin feature/login-tests
realWorldExample: An SDET creates a branch `fix/stale-element` to update flaky locators. After committing and pushing, they open a Pull Request. Another SDET reviews the code, approves it, and it merges into `main`.
commonMistakes: Committing hardcoded passwords or API keys to the repository.
Committing compiled files (`.class`) or report folders (`test-output/`) because of a missing `.gitignore`.
Working directly on the `main` branch instead of using feature branches.
bestPractices: Keep commits small and focused on a single logical change.
Write descriptive commit messages (e.g., 'Fix login timeout issue' instead of 'updates').
Perform code reviews on Pull Requests to maintain test framework quality.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between `git pull` and `git fetch`?
interviewQuestions.a: `git fetch` downloads new data from a remote repository but doesn't integrate it into your working files. `git pull` is essentially `git fetch` followed by `git merge`, directly updating your current branch.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you resolve a merge conflict?
interviewQuestions.a: Open the conflicting files, look for the conflict markers (`<<<<`, `====`, `>>>>`), manually edit the file to keep the desired changes, save, `git add` the resolved files, and `git commit`.

revisionSummary: Git lifecycle: Working Directory -> (add) -> Staging -> (commit) -> Local Repo -> (push) -> Remote Repo. Always branch, commit small, and use .gitignore.
